Most states use winner-take-all allocation of electoral votes. The Electoral College creates strategic incentives, focusing campaigns on swing states while making many states uncompetitive. Critics argue the Electoral College can produce a president who loses the popular vote (as in 2000 and 2016), overrepresents small states, and discourages turnout in non-competitive states. Defenders argue it preserves federalism and ensures geographic breadth of support. Students must analyze the presidential election process and evaluate arguments for and against the Electoral College. AP exam note: Electoral College mechanics and reform debates are heavily tested. Vocabulary: Electoral College, primary election, caucus, swing state, winner-take-all, delegate, national convention, popular vote, battleground state, faithless elector.

Political Participation.skill: PMI-5.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.8) Political Participation - 5.8 Congressional Elections
Political Participation.prompt: This topic covers congressional elections for AP US Government and Politics students (CED: PMI-5.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.8). Students should understand the key features and dynamics of congressional elections. House members represent single-member districts redrawn every ten years following the census (reapportionment and redistricting). Gerrymandering (drawing district boundaries to advantage a party or group) is a significant concern, with partisan gerrymandering creating safe seats and reducing electoral competition. Baker v. Carr (1962) established that redistricting is a justiciable (court-reviewable) issue, leading to the 'one person, one vote' principle (Reynolds v. Sims, 1964; Wesberry v. Sanders, 1964). Senate races are statewide contests with higher visibility and cost. Incumbency advantage is strong in both chambers, driven by name recognition, fundraising advantages, franking privilege, and credit claiming. Midterm elections typically result in the president's party losing seats. Students must analyze how structural features of congressional elections affect representation and competition, and evaluate the impact of gerrymandering on democratic governance. AP exam note: Baker v. Carr is a required Supreme Court case. Vocabulary: reapportionment, redistricting, gerrymandering, incumbent, midterm election, safe seat, open seat, one person one vote, Baker v. Carr, franking privilege.

Political Participation.skill: PMI-5.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.9) Political Participation - 5.9 Modern Campaigns
Political Participation.prompt: This topic covers modern campaigns for AP US Government and Politics students (CED: PMI-5.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.9). Students should understand how modern political campaigns operate and how they have evolved. Campaigns have become candidate-centered rather than party-centered, relying on professional consultants, pollsters, media strategists, and data analytics. Campaign strategies include building name recognition, defining the candidate's message and the opponent's weaknesses, targeting persuadable voters through microtargeting (using data to identify and reach specific voter segments), and mobilizing the base through get-out-the-vote (GOTV) efforts. Television advertising remains important, but digital media and social media have transformed campaigning by enabling direct communication with voters, rapid fundraising, and viral content. The permanent campaign phenomenon means elected officials begin fundraising and positioning for reelection immediately after winning. Students must analyze how modern campaign strategies affect voter engagement and democratic outcomes, and evaluate the impact of technology on the campaign process. Connect to campaign finance, media coverage, and voter behavior. AP exam note: understanding campaign strategies and their impact on elections is important. Vocabulary: candidate-centered campaign, microtargeting, GOTV, opposition research, media consultant, pollster, swing voter, base, permanent campaign, digital campaigning.

Political Participation.skill: PMI-5.D (Unit 5, Topic 5.10) Political Participation - 5.10 Campaign Finance
Political Participation.prompt: This topic covers campaign finance for AP US Government and Politics students (CED: PMI-5.D, Unit 5, Topic 5.10). Students should understand the legal framework governing campaign finance and the ongoing debate over money in politics. Key legislation includes the Federal Election Campaign Act (FECA, 1971) and its amendments, which established the Federal Election Commission (FEC), disclosure requirements, and contribution limits. Buckley v. Valeo (1976) upheld contribution limits but struck down spending limits, ruling that political spending is a form of protected free speech. The Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (McCain-Feingold, 2002) banned soft money contributions to parties and restricted issue ads before elections. Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010) held that corporations and unions have First Amendment rights to make independent expenditures in elections, leading to the rise of Super PACs (independent expenditure-only committees that can raise unlimited funds). Students must analyze how campaign finance laws balance free speech with preventing corruption, and evaluate the impact of Citizens United on elections. AP exam note: Citizens United v. FEC is a required Supreme Court case. Vocabulary: PAC, Super PAC, soft money, hard money, FEC, disclosure, contribution limits, independent expenditure, dark money, Citizens United, Buckley v. Valeo.

Political Participation.skill: MPA-4.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.11) Political Participation - 5.11 The Media
Political Participation.prompt: This topic covers the media for AP US Government and Politics students (CED: MPA-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.11). Students should understand the role of media in American democracy and how it influences political processes. The media performs several democratic functions: informing the public (watchdog role), setting the political agenda (determining which issues receive attention), framing issues (how stories are presented affects public perception), and serving as a forum for political debate. Agenda setting refers to the media's power to influence what issues the public considers important, while framing refers to how the presentation of an issue shapes understanding and opinion. The media also acts as a gatekeeper, filtering which stories and perspectives reach the public. Horse-race journalism focuses on campaign polling and strategy rather than policy substance. The relationship between politicians and the media involves mutual dependence: politicians need coverage, and media need access and content. Students must analyze how media coverage affects public opinion, campaigns, and governance, and evaluate the media's effectiveness in supporting democratic accountability. Connect to press freedom, political communication, and media bias. AP exam note: analyzing media effects on public opinion is a key analytical skill. Vocabulary: agenda setting, framing, gatekeeping, horse-race journalism, watchdog, media bias, priming, sound bite, spin, media consolidation.

Political Participation.skill: MPA-4.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.12) Political Participation - 5.12 Changing Media
Political Participation.prompt: This topic covers the changing media landscape for AP US Government and Politics students (CED: MPA-4.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.12). Students should understand how technological changes have transformed political communication and media consumption. The shift from traditional media (network television, newspapers, radio) to digital and social media (online news, blogs, social media platforms, podcasts) has fragmented the media landscape and created echo chambers where people consume information that reinforces existing beliefs. The decline of local journalism reduces accountability at the state and local level. The rise of partisan media outlets has contributed to political polarization, as consumers self-select into ideologically consistent information environments (selective exposure). Misinformation and disinformation spread rapidly through social media, challenging informed democratic participation. The 24-hour news cycle accelerates coverage but may reduce depth and accuracy. Citizens now have unprecedented ability to create and share political content directly. Students must analyze how the changing media environment affects political discourse, public opinion formation, and democratic processes, and evaluate the implications of media fragmentation for American democracy. AP exam note: analyzing the impact of new media on political participation is commonly tested. Vocabulary: echo chamber, filter bubble, selective exposure, partisan media, misinformation, disinformation, media fragmentation, citizen journalism, social media, 24-hour news cycle, digital divide.
